title: Movement Quality, Joint Health & Mechanics
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Tapp Brothers or Jesse La Flair (parkour tutorials)

competence_markers.description: The student can explain the joint-by-joint approach and identify which joints need mobility and which need stability
The student performs a daily mobility routine before training without being reminded
The student can perform 10+ full push-ups, 3+ strict pull-ups, and hold a 60-second plank with good form
The student can hold a wall handstand for 30+ seconds
The student can perform a full-depth squat (hips below knees, heels on ground) and hold it for 2+ minutes
The student can perform a precise forward roll, backward roll, and cartwheel in a straight line
The student can perform a safety vault and precision jump at knee-to-waist height obstacles
The student can swim 200+ yards continuously in freestyle
The student can tread water for 10 minutes
The student can describe the difference between flexibility and mobility and explain why mobility matters more
The student warms up and cools down independently — this is a habit, not a reminder

cross_connections.description: The movement quality developed here makes barbell training safe. A student who squats with full depth and good mechanics is ready for load.
Tumbling, falling, body awareness, and agility from parkour and gymnastics directly transfer to the mat.
The daily mobility practice is an IC2 discipline exercise — it is boring, it does not produce visible results quickly, and it is essential. The student who does it anyway is building discipline.
The joint-by-joint framework is applied anatomy and biomechanics. Understanding why your body works the way it does is science applied to yourself.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned to move. You built a vocabulary — crawling, climbing, jumping, rolling, hanging, swimming. You became comfortable with your body in motion.

Now you are going to learn *why* your body moves the way it does, and how to make it move better. You are going to learn about your joints — which ones need to be mobile and which ones need to be stable. You are going to learn the difference between flexibility and mobility. You are going to practice bodyweight skills that demand real control — handstands, pull-up progressions, pistol squat progressions — and you are going to develop the habit of taking care of your body through mobility work and warm-ups that are not optional add-ons but essential parts of training.

This matters because movement quality determines everything else. A person who moves well can train hard without getting injured, perform any sport efficiently, and maintain physical capability for decades. A person who moves poorly gets hurt, plateaus early, and breaks down. Most injuries in training and sport are not caused by bad luck. They are caused by bad movement — loading a pattern that is not ready for load. This packet teaches you to identify and fix those patterns before they become problems.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding of the joint-by-joint approach** — which joints need mobility and which need stability
2. **Mobility as a practice** — foam rolling, band work, loaded stretching, active range of motion work
3. **Bodyweight movement progressions** — structured paths to pull-ups, dips, handstands, pistol squats, and L-sits
4. **Tumbling and gymnastics basics** — precise forward and backward rolls, cartwheels, handstands
5. **Parkour fundamentals** — precision jumps, vaults, wall runs in controlled environments
6. **Advanced swimming** — distance swimming, extended treading, diving, rescue basics, open water introduction
7. **Running mechanics** — technical framework for efficient running at all speeds
8. **The habit of warm-up and cool-down** — performed independently, without being told
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Joint-by-Joint Approach — How Your Body Is Designed

Your body is a stack of joints, and those joints alternate between needing two different qualities:

- **Mobility** — the ability to move through a large range of motion with control
- **Stability** — the ability to resist unwanted movement under load

Starting from the ground up:
- **Ankle** — needs MOBILITY (the ability to flex and extend fully)
- **Knee** — needs STABILITY (should track straight, resist collapsing inward or outward)
- **Hip** — needs MOBILITY (should move freely in all directions — flexion, extension, rotation)
- **Lumbar spine (lower back)** — needs STABILITY (should resist excessive flexion, extension, and rotation)
- **Thoracic spine (upper back)** — needs MOBILITY (should extend and rotate freely)
- **Scapula (shoulder blade)** — needs STABILITY (should stay flat against the ribcage and move in controlled patterns)
- **Shoulder** — needs MOBILITY (the most mobile joint in the body — should move freely in all directions)

**Why this matters:** Most pain and injury patterns come from violating this alternation. When a mobile joint becomes stiff, the stable joint above or below it is forced to move to compensate — and that joint is not designed for movement, so it gets hurt.

Example: Tight hips → the lower back is forced to flex and rotate during movements like squatting or running → lower back pain. The problem is not the back — it is the hips. Fix hip mobility and the back pain resolves.

Example: Stiff thoracic spine → the shoulder or lower back compensates → shoulder impingement or lumbar pain during overhead movements.

This framework will govern how you approach every physical skill for the rest of your life. When something hurts, ask: which joint is stiff that is forcing the painful joint to do work it should not be doing?

### Part 2: Mobility Work as a Daily Practice

Mobility is not stretching. Stretching is passive — someone pushes you further. Mobility is active — you own the range of motion with muscular control. A person who can do a split but cannot kick to head height has flexibility without mobility. A person who can actively lift their leg to head height has mobility. Mobility is what matters for performance and injury prevention.

**Daily mobility practice (15–20 minutes):**

- **Foam rolling** — roll your calves, quads, IT band, glutes, upper back, and lats. Spend 60–90 seconds on each area. When you find a tender spot, pause on it for 10–20 seconds. Foam rolling reduces tissue density and prepares the muscle for stretching and movement.
- **Ankle mobility** — knee-over-toe lunges against a wall. Drive your knee forward past your toes while keeping your heel on the ground. You need at least 4 inches of knee travel past your toes for a proper squat.
- **Hip mobility** — 90/90 position transitions (sit with both legs at 90-degree angles, rotate from one side to the other), deep squat hold (sit in the bottom of a squat with heels on the ground for 2+ minutes), pigeon stretch, hip circles.
- **Thoracic spine mobility** — foam roller extensions (lie with the roller across your upper back and extend over it), seated rotations, wall slides for scapular control.
- **Shoulder mobility** — band pull-aparts, shoulder dislocates with a dowel or band (slowly rotating a stick from in front of you to behind you with straight arms), wall slides.

**The rule:** Do your mobility work before you train. Every time. Not some of the time. Every time. A warm, mobile body trains better and gets hurt less. This is not negotiable.

### Part 3: Bodyweight Movement Progressions

These are structured paths from basic movements to advanced skills. You find where you are on each progression and work to the next level.

**Push progression:**
Wall push-ups → Incline push-ups → Knee push-ups → Full push-ups → Diamond push-ups → Archer push-ups → Decline push-ups → Dips (parallel bars) → Pseudo-planche push-ups

**Pull progression:**
Dead hang → Active hang → Flexed-arm hang → Negative pull-ups (jump up, lower slowly) → Band-assisted pull-ups → Full pull-ups → Chest-to-bar pull-ups → L-sit pull-ups → Weighted pull-ups

**Squat progression:**
Air squat → Goblet squat hold → Deep squat hold (2 minutes) → Bulgarian split squat → Assisted pistol squat (holding something for balance) → Full pistol squat

**Handstand progression:**
Wall plank (feet on wall, hands on floor) → Wall handstand (face to wall) → Wall handstand hold (60 seconds) → Freestanding handstand attempts → Freestanding handstand hold → Handstand walk

**Core progression:**
Hollow body hold → Plank (60+ seconds) → Side plank → Hanging knee raises → Hanging leg raises → L-sit on parallettes → L-sit on floor

**How to use these progressions:** Find the hardest variation you can do with good form for 5 repetitions (or 15 seconds for holds). That is your working level. Practice it 3–4 times per week. When you can do 3 sets of 8 reps (or 3 sets of 30 seconds for holds), move to the next variation. Do not skip levels. Do not sacrifice form for progression.

### Part 4: Tumbling and Gymnastics Basics

You learned basic rolling in Stretching Branches. Now you refine those rolls and add new skills.

- **Precise forward roll** — from standing, tuck and roll in a straight line, stand up in exactly the same line you started. No veering sideways. Hands barely touch the ground.
- **Precise backward roll** — same standard. Straight line. Stand up cleanly.
- **Cartwheel** — hand-hand-foot-foot in a straight line. Start against a wall for balance if needed. Practice both directions — your non-dominant side needs work.
- **Wall handstand** — kick up to a handstand against the wall. Hold. Build time. This develops the shoulder strength and body awareness for freestanding handstands.
- **Freestanding handstand attempts** — kick up away from the wall. Fall, try again. Use a spotter or practice on grass. The handstand is one of the most revealing bodyweight skills — it requires shoulder mobility, core control, balance, and courage simultaneously.

### Part 5: Parkour Fundamentals

Parkour is the discipline of moving through an environment efficiently — getting from point A to point B using whatever obstacles are in the way. It is not about flashy flips. It is about reading an environment and choosing the most efficient path through it.

**Fundamental skills:**

- **Precision jump** — jump to a specific target (a rail, a ledge, a line) and stick the landing. Both feet, quiet, balanced. This is the foundation. If you cannot land precisely, you cannot vault safely.
- **Safety vault** — one hand on the obstacle, both legs swing over to the same side. The simplest and safest vault. Practice on low walls (knee to waist height).
- **Speed vault** — one hand on the obstacle, body passes over with minimal hand contact. Faster than the safety vault. Practice only after the safety vault is solid.
- **Kong vault** — both hands on the obstacle, body passes between the arms with legs tucked. More athletic than the safety vault. Requires shoulder strength and timing.
- **Wall run** — run at a wall, plant one foot on the wall, push off to reach the top. Start with low walls. The technique is in the foot placement and the timing of the push.
- **Underbar** — swing feet-first through a gap between rails or under a bar. Requires momentum control and the ability to release and re-grab.

**Safety principles:** Always practice new skills at low heights first. Always check the surface for wetness, loose material, or damage before vaulting. Always have an exit plan — know where you will go if the movement fails. Never practice alone when learning new skills.

### Part 6: Swimming — Weathering Storms

- **Distance swimming** — build to 200+ yards (8 pool lengths) continuous freestyle. Then backstroke. Then alternating strokes. This is aerobic conditioning and stroke refinement combined.
- **Extended treading** — 10 minutes of treading water. Build endurance progressively. This is the survival standard — a person who can tread for 10 minutes can survive almost any accidental water entry.
- **Diving** — proper dive entry from the deck (arms extended, head between arms, enter at an angle). Surface dive (swimming down from the surface to depth).
- **Rescue swimming basics** — reach, throw, go (with a flotation device). How to approach a drowning person without getting pulled under. The cross-chest carry.
- **Open water introduction** — supervised swimming in a lake, river, or ocean. Understanding currents, waves, temperature, and the difference between pool swimming and open water.

### Part 7: Running Mechanics

Running looks simple but has a specific technical framework that makes it efficient and injury-resistant.

**Key principles:**
- **Midfoot strike** — land on the middle of your foot, not your heel. Heel striking is a brake. Midfoot striking is smooth and efficient.
- **Cadence** — aim for 170–180 steps per minute. This sounds fast, but it means shorter, quicker steps — not longer strides. Higher cadence reduces impact force per step.
- **Forward lean** — lean from the ankles (not the waist). Gravity assists your forward motion.
- **Compact arms** — elbows at roughly 90 degrees, hands relaxed, arms driving forward and back (not across the body).

**Sprint mechanics** differ from distance running: higher knee drive, more aggressive arm pump, greater forward lean, and a forefoot rather than midfoot strike. Sprint and distance running are different skills — practice both.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Joint-by-Joint Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can name the alternating pattern (ankle=mobile, knee=stable, etc.) without prompting. When something is tight or painful, they can reason about which joint might be the source of the problem. They apply this framework to their own body.

**Developing slowly:** Can recite the pattern from memory but does not apply it. Cannot connect their own tight hips to their lower back discomfort.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you discussed specific examples from their own training? Abstract knowledge becomes real when applied to their body. "Your back hurt during squats yesterday — which joint upstream might be stiff?"

### 2. Independent Mobility Practice
**Developing well:** Does 15–20 minutes of mobility work before every training session without being told. Has a consistent routine. Can modify it based on what feels tight that day.

**Developing slowly:** Skips mobility unless reminded. Does it perfunctorily — going through the motions without engaging. Cannot explain why they are doing each component.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled the behavior? If you skip your own warm-up, the student notices. Are you training alongside them? Shared mobility practice is more sustainable than assigned mobility practice.

### 3. Bodyweight Progressions
**Developing well:** Can perform 10+ push-ups, 3+ pull-ups, 60-second plank, 30-second wall handstand. Working actively on the next level in each progression. Form is clean.

**Developing slowly:** Stuck at early levels. Form breaks down under fatigue. Avoids progressions they find difficult.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student training these skills 3–4 times per week? Bodyweight skills require frequency. Is form the priority, or are they chasing numbers? A student doing 5 perfect push-ups is more advanced than a student doing 15 sloppy ones.

### 4. Parkour Safety and Skill
**Developing well:** Assesses surfaces before vaulting. Practices at appropriate heights. Can perform safety vault and precision jump reliably. Progresses methodically.

**Developing slowly:** Attempts skills beyond their level. Skips assessment. Falls or fails frequently due to overconfidence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student separating the desire to look cool from the discipline of progressive training? Parkour attracts ego. The student who practices precision jumps for weeks before attempting vaults is training correctly. The student who attempts a kong vault on day one is training their ego.

### 5. Swimming Competence
**Developing well:** Swims 200+ yards continuously. Treads for 10 minutes. Comfortable in open water under supervision. Can demonstrate basic rescue approach.

**Developing slowly:** Still limited to short distances. Uncomfortable in deep water. Avoids open water.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough pool time? Swimming competence requires volume. Once per week is maintenance; twice per week is development.
